title: Na-K-ATPase
body_html:
Background
The Na혖쨘/K혖쨘-ATPase, Na혖쨘/K혖쨘 pump, Na-K-ATPase or sodium-potassium ATPase is vital for maintaining the concentration gradient between the main cations of the intracellular and extracellular space.

The Na혖쨘/K혖쨘-ATPase is an active pump that consumes energy obtained from the conversion of ATP to ADP.

The main function of the Na혖쨘/K혖쨘-ATPase is to maintain cell resting potential. Cells achieve this by keeping large sodium and potassium concentration gradients between the intracellular and extracellular fluid. Typical concentrations are shown in the table below:

IonIntracellular concentrationExtracellular concentration
Sodium (Na+)10mmol/l140mmol
Potassium (K+)140 mmol/l4mmol/l

Sodium and potassium ions will move between the intracellular and extracellular space through ion channels in the cell membrane via passive diffusion along their concentration gradients. If left unopposed this would result in the eventual equalisation of sodium and potassium concentrations in and outside of cells which would be catastrophic for cell electrical signalling. The Na혖쨘/K혖쨘-ATPase works antagonistically to the natural movement of these ions by actively pumping Na+ and K+ against their concentration gradients.

The Na혖쨘/K혖쨘-ATPase pumps 3 sodium ions from the intracellular space into the extracellular space in exchange for 2 potassium ions which move in the reverse direction. Both ions are moved against their concentration gradients. click on image to enlarge

Schematic representation of the Na혖쨘/K혖쨘-ATPase pump. click on image to enlarge

Key Points
  • The Na혖쨘/K혖쨘-ATPase pump is vital for maintaining the concentration gradient of the major intracellular and extracellular cations potassium and sodium
  • The Na혖쨘/K혖쨘-ATPase pump moves ions against their concentration gradient.
  • This is an active process requiring energy released from the hydrolysis and phosphorylation of ATP to ADP.
  • The pump binds 3 sodium ions for 2 potassium ions for each ATP hydrolysis.
